erse=E2=80=94protecting the cable asset, launching the Flagship lifeboat, b=
uttressing the broader Disney family of O.T.T. products=E2=80=94chairman Ji=
mmy Pitaro seems to have at least settled on a highly nuanced talent strate=
gy. Pitaro and his top lieutenant, Burke Magnus, have eliminated a tier of =
pricey and not totally necessary stars (Steve Young, Jeff Van Gundy, Max Ke=
llerman, Tony Reali, etcetera); signed and protected a category of more ver=
satile talent (Dan Orlovsky, Mina Kimes, Malika Andrews, plus newbies like =
Jason Kelce, Nick Saban, and Bill Belichick); and placed some big bets on=
=E2=80=94and this is such a Bristol term=E2=80=94the needle-movers.

Needle movers have dedicated personal
audiences, the ability to generate ratings, and the appeal to lead their fa=
ns to a new O.T.T. service. They=E2=80=99re guys like Mike Greenberg and Sc=
ott Van Pelt, who helm hours of general interest TV and have individual spo=
rt expertise. Needle-movers are game announcers, like Kirk Herbstreit, Joe =
Buck, and Troy Aikman, who add unique value to prized media rights relation=
ships, as well as insiders like Adam Schefter. Pat McAfee, and his $17 mill=
ion production deal, is on that list, too, despite the headaches he causes.=
 So are the Manning brothers. But Stephen A. Smith is the biggest of them a=
ll.

=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F6%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+=
SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F6%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustom=
er.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

Stephen A., whose contract expires within the next six months, is arguably =
the biggest star in a contract year in ESPN history. And as I reported earl=
ier this year, he was looking to potentially double (and then some) his cur=
rent $12 million salary in a deal worth more than $25 million per year. Giv=
en the vanity and egomania that pervades the industry, it was hardly a surp=
rise that Stephen A. wanted to make more than his colleagues, have a reason=
ably flexible deal structure, and sort of get to do whatever the hell he wa=
nted=E2=80=94like plug into ESPN=E2=80=99s NFL relationship, for instance. =
A generation ago, ESPN executives might have let this sort of talent test t=
he open market or depart for the verdant pastures of CNNSI, FS1, or even MS=
NBC. But not in the needle-mover era.

Stephen A. for Effort

As Marchand reported in The Athletic, Stephen A. and ESPN are inching towar=
d a deal currently structured at five years and around $20 million per. In =
many ways, it=E2=80=99s a perfect Goldilocks arrangement: Stephen A. will b=
e the brand=E2=80=99s highest-paid star, but ESPN is not on the hook for th=
e entire salary. ESPN Bet has committed to pay several million dollars of t=
hat salary each year. Disney will chip in, too, since the deal specifies th=
at Stephen A. can pitch non-sports ideas to the parentco.

Sources said that Stephen A. would continue with First Take and would provi=
de content for ESPN Bet. ESPN has also agreed to identify opportunities for=
 Stephen A. to contribute to ESPN=E2=80=99s NFL and NBA coverage, though th=
at arrangement appears to be less formal than originally thought. Sources t=
ell me that the two sides are close to signing
an agreement, but a few issues still remain. None of those issues is expect=
ed to derail the deal, but they could delay it when it ultimately closes.

More importantly, the Stephen A. pact is non-exclusive outside of sports co=
ntent. If the former Philadelphia Inquirer journalist wants to pursue a pat=
h toward broader cultural stardom via projects outside sports, he=E2=80=99l=
l have Pitaro=E2=80=99s green light. For instance, there are persistent rum=
ors that SiriusXM has put forth a considerable bid for him to host a radio =
show that would cover similar topics to his current YouTube show. Could Ste=
phen A., who has appeared on Fox News, become some sort of Imus?

All we can say for sure is that this type of concession would have been uni=
maginable just a few short years ago, in the Skipper era, when ESPN positio=
ned itself as the center of the sports media universe. Alas, now all the la=
rge-platform companies getting into sports have
prompted some creativity. Pitaro and Magnus clearly believe that they are i=
n a battle for on-air talent, not just from competing sports networks, but =
from the streamers and social media outlets. As a result, they have become =
much more lenient about letting their stars produce non-sports programming =
on other platforms. I don=E2=80=99t know about you, but Marchand can=E2=80=
=99t wait for the Stephen A. baking show and harpsichord YouTube channel.

er it was an orientation problem=E2=80=94whether they were
too left and needed to be more centrist. In fact, that wasn=E2=80=99t the p=

u can=E2=80=99t admit that you=E2=80=99ve got a severe problem, then you=E2=
=80=99re stuck. =E2=80=A6 Another obvious change that=E2=80=99s interesting=
 to watch play out in media is that people feel less constrained by what ot=
her people think of them, by cancel culture, by whatever fucking <i>woke</i=
> is. We=E2=80=99re gonna see a reckless abandon in the kind of content peo=
ple are making, left and right. It=E2=80=99s a breath of fresh air to see p=
eople with points of view. I want more of that in media. You don=E2=80=99t =
have to be right 100 percent of the time, but you have to be interesting, a=
nd you have to be entertaining, and hopefully you=E2=80=99re an expert at s=
omething.=E2=80=9D </li></ul>

r Lorenz </b>came under fire for ostensibly celebrating the murder of <b>Br=

nhattan on Wednesday. =E2=80=9CAnd people wonder why we want these executiv=
es dead,=E2=80=9D Lorenz, stunningly, wrote on social media. She also share=
d celebratory graphics relating to the murder of the 50-year-old father of =
two, and posted the full name, title, and headshot of another health insura=
nce executive. Lorenz later wrote an essay <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck=
=2Enews/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1l=
EQUt2UktxclJLZ0dUbnBQcmJudFhnZHFrN2FhQ=

63d1d1711d8359e078eb5eb694021328840bd8c6b129951" style=3D"color:#d40000;tex=
t-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">defending herself</a></u>=

teresting to see her show her true character after the <i>Times</i> and the=

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">As <b>Marchand </b>=
reported in The Athletic, Stephen A. and ESPN are inching toward a deal cur=
rently structured at five years and around $20 million per. In many ways, i=
t=E2=80=99s a perfect Goldilocks arrangement: Stephen A. will be the brand=
=E2=80=99s highest-paid star, but ESPN is not on the hook for the entire sa=
lary. ESPN Bet has committed to pay several million dollars of that salary =
each year. Disney will chip in, too, since the deal specifies that Stephen =
A. can pitch non-sports ideas to the parentco. <br/><br/>Sources said that =
Stephen A. would continue with <i>First Take</i> and would provide content =
for ESPN Bet. ESPN has also agreed to identify opportunities for Stephen A.=
 to contribute to ESPN=E2=80=99s NFL and NBA coverage, though that arrangem=
ent appears to be less formal than originally thought. Sources tell me that=
 the two sides are close to signing
an agreement, but a few issues still remain. None of those issues is expect=
ed to derail the deal, but they could delay it when it ultimately closes.<b=
r/><br/>More importantly, the Stephen A. pact is <i>non-exclusive </i>outsi=
de of sports content. If the former <i>Philadelphia Inquirer </i>journalist=
 wants to pursue a path toward broader cultural stardom via projects <i>out=
side </i>sports, he=E2=80=99ll have Pitaro=E2=80=99s green light. For insta=
nce, there are persistent rumors that SiriusXM has put forth a considerable=
 bid for him to host a radio show that would cover similar topics to his cu=
rrent YouTube show. Could Stephen A., who has appeared on Fox News, become =
some sort of <b>Imus</b>? <br/><br/>All we can say for sure is that this ty=
pe of concession would have been <i>unimaginable</i> just a few short years=
 ago, in the <b>Skipper </b>era, when ESPN positioned itself as the center =
of the sports media universe. Alas, now all the large-platform companies ge=
tting into sports have
prompted some creativity. Pitaro and Magnus clearly believe that they are i=
n a battle for on-air talent, not just from competing sports networks, but =
from the streamers and social media outlets. As a result, they have become =
much more lenient about letting their stars produce non-sports programming =
on other platforms. I don=E2=80=99t know about you, but Marchand can=E2=80=
=99t wait for the Stephen A. baking show and harpsichord YouTube channel. <=
br/></td>
